The mechanism: advocates first make an idea 'thinkable' (articulating it at all), then 'acceptable' (defending it in serious debate), then 'sensible' (making it seem reasonable), then 'popular' (getting majority support), then 'policy' (legislating it). Understanding the Overton Window is critical for: thinking clearly about which ideas are rejected because they're genuinely bad versus because they're outside the current window, and understanding how social change actually happens over time.

name: Semantic Search
description: Traditional search engines find documents that contain the exact words you typed — keyword search. If you search 'car' you don't find 'automobile' unless the document happened to use both words. Semantic search uses vector embeddings to find documents by meaning, not keywords: it converts both your query and all documents into points in a meaning-space, then returns the documents that are closest in meaning to your query — even if they use completely different words. Search for 'how to deal with a difficult boss' and semantic search finds articles about 'managing up' and 'workplace conflict resolution' that share the meaning without matching the words. This is how modern AI assistants answer questions about your documents — they convert your question and all your documents into vectors, find the most relevant passages by geometric proximity, then use those passages to formulate an answer.
linked_nodes: The Transformer (Attention is All You Need)

name: The Compound Learning Rate
description: Learning is not linear — it compounds. A person who learns 1% better each day for a year ends up 37 times better than when they started (1.01^365 ≈ 37.8). A person who learns 0.5% better each day ends up only 6 times better. The 0.5% gap in daily improvement produces a 6x gap in annual outcomes. This is why consistent, even modest, daily learning investment produces extraordinary long-term advantage — not because any individual day's learning is remarkable, but because the gains stack multiplicatively. What accelerates the compound learning rate? Deliberate practice targeting actual weaknesses, spaced repetition to prevent forgetting, building on deep foundations rather than surface familiarity, and connecting new ideas to existing knowledge structures. Every mentor, every clarifying conversation, every well-designed learning environment slightly raises the exponent.

name: Perspective-Taking Exercises
description: Perspective-taking is not the same as empathy (feeling what someone feels) — it's the cognitive practice of accurately imagining the world from inside another person's situation, values, and knowledge state. Research by Adam Galinsky found that perspective-taking improves negotiation outcomes, reduces stereotyping, and increases creativity. The practice: before any important interaction, pause and genuinely reconstruct the other person's situation — not 'if I were in their shoes' (you'd bring your own values), but 'given their background, their fears, their goals, and their knowledge, what makes sense from where they stand?' This is hard because it requires temporarily suspending your own framework. The test: can you articulate the other person's position so compellingly that they'd say 'yes, exactly' — not your interpretation of their position, but their actual position as they experience it?

name: Password Security & Storage
description: Most people reuse the same 3-5 passwords across dozens of services. When one service gets breached (and many do — billions of credentials are available on dark-web markets), attackers run 'credential stuffing' attacks: they try your username and password from the breached site on every other major service. If you reused the password, they're in. The correct defense has three components: unique passwords for every service (making breach of one site contain the damage to that site), passwords that are long and random (not guessable by dictionary or pattern), and a password manager to remember all of them. On the server side, passwords should never be stored in plaintext or even reversible encrypted form — only as bcrypt or Argon2 hashes with random per-user 'salts' (added random data before hashing, so identical passwords produce different hashes, defeating precomputed hash tables).

name: OPSEC (Operational Security)
description: OPSEC was developed by the US military during the Vietnam War when commanders realized that individually innocuous information — troop mealtimes, laundry schedules, vehicle maintenance patterns — could be assembled by an adversary into a picture of operational plans. OPSEC is the process of identifying what sensitive information you have, who might want it and what they'd do with it, what vulnerabilities could expose it, and what countermeasures to take. Applied to personal digital security: the Instagram photo showing your boarding pass reveals your travel dates and destination. The geotag on your regular 'office coffee' photo reveals where you work. Your Amazon wishlist reveals your address. Your LinkedIn reveals your employer, boss's name, and daily schedule. Each data point is harmless; the aggregate is a surveillance profile. OPSEC thinking treats information aggregation as the real threat.

name: Data Privacy & GDPR
description: Before 2018, companies could collect, sell, and process personal data with almost no restriction. Europe's GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) changed the global landscape: any company that processes data about EU residents must comply, regardless of where the company is located. The core rights: right to know what data is collected, right to access it, right to correct it, right to erasure ('the right to be forgotten'), right to data portability, and the right not to be subject to automated decision-making without human review. Companies must have a legal basis for processing personal data (consent, contract necessity, legitimate interest), and consent must be specific, informed, and freely given — pre-ticked boxes don't count. GDPR's fines are severe: up to 4% of global annual revenue. This established that data has owner rights, not just business rights.

name: IP Addresses & Routing
description: Every device on the internet has an IP (Internet Protocol) address — a number that identifies it. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (like 192.168.1.1), giving about 4 billion possible addresses — nearly exhausted by the explosion of connected devices. IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses (quadrillions × quadrillions of addresses). When you send data, it travels in packets across a network of routers — specialized computers whose only job is to look at the destination IP of each arriving packet and decide which direction to forward it (like relay race batons passing through a maze of junctions). The routing protocol (BGP — Border Gateway Protocol) allows routers to share maps of the network so they can make these forwarding decisions in milliseconds. A BGP misconfiguration in 2008 accidentally took YouTube offline globally — illustrating how fragile the routing infrastructure of the entire internet actually is.

name: VPNs & Tunneling
description: A VPN (Virtual Private Network) creates an encrypted 'tunnel' between your device and a VPN server. All your traffic flows through this tunnel: your ISP sees encrypted data going to the VPN server, not your actual requests. The destination websites see the VPN server's IP address, not yours. This solves specific problems: preventing your ISP from monitoring or selling your browsing history, hiding your real location from services, and accessing content blocked in your country. What a VPN doesn't do: make you anonymous (the VPN provider can see your traffic), protect you from malware or phishing, or make HTTP websites secure. The privacy model just shifts: instead of trusting your ISP, you trust the VPN provider. For journalism, whistleblowing, or bypassing censorship, VPNs combined with Tor (which routes traffic through multiple layers of encryption and nodes) provide much stronger anonymity.

name: Defense in Depth
description: Medieval castles didn't have just one wall — they had a moat, an outer wall, an inner wall, a keep, and as the attacker penetrated each layer, the defenders fell back to the next. Defense in depth is the same principle applied to security: no single security control is assumed to be perfect, so you layer multiple independent controls such that an attacker must defeat all of them simultaneously. A web application with defense in depth has: a WAF (Web Application Firewall) blocking common attacks at the network edge, input validation preventing malicious data from entering the application, parameterized queries preventing SQL injection at the database layer, least-privilege accounts limiting damage even if a service is compromised, and monitoring/alerting to detect breaches early. The layered model means a failure in any single layer doesn't automatically produce a breach — the other layers must also fail simultaneously.

name: Nudge Theory
description: Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein showed that the way choices are presented — the 'choice architecture' — dramatically influences what people choose, without restricting their freedom. Defaulting employees into pension plans (with opt-out) instead of requiring opt-in increases enrollment from ~15% to ~90% — same freedom, different default. Placing fruit at eye level in cafeteria lines increases fruit consumption significantly without removing junk food. These are nudges: interventions that 'predictably alter behavior without forbidding options or significantly changing economic incentives.' Governments and organizations worldwide have established 'nudge units' to redesign choice architectures for better public health, tax compliance, energy efficiency, and financial savings outcomes. The ethical debate: nudges are pervasive anyway (every default choice is a nudge) — the question is whether they're designed deliberately with or without awareness.

name: Behavioral Economics Foundations
description: Classical economics assumes humans are rational, self-interested, and consistent — the legendary 'Homo economicus.' Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky systematically dismantled this assumption. They found humans are predictably irrational: we have two systems of thought (System 1: fast, automatic, emotional; System 2: slow, deliberate, logical), we rely on heuristics that produce systematic biases, we value losses far more than equivalent gains (loss aversion), we prefer the status quo (status quo bias), we anchor on irrelevant numbers (anchoring bias), and our preferences are context-dependent and inconsistent. The power of behavioral economics is that the irrationality is *systematic* — predictable and therefore exploitable (by marketers and dark pattern designers) or correctable (by good mechanism and choice architecture design).

name: Cognitive Biases Stack
description: Your brain runs on approximately 200+ known cognitive biases — systematic patterns of deviation from rational judgment. They aren't random errors; they're predictable shortcuts (heuristics) that were useful in the ancestral environment but misfire in the modern one. The most dangerous property of cognitive biases is that they are largely invisible to the person experiencing them — you feel like you're reasoning clearly when you're actually being systematically distorted. Key architecture: biases cluster into categories. Biases related to what you SEE (availability heuristic, attentional bias). Biases about what you BELIEVE (confirmation bias, belief perseverance). Biases about HOW YOU DECIDE (loss aversion, sunk cost, status quo bias). Biases about SOCIAL situations (in-group bias, authority bias, social proof). Becoming fluent in the major categories — not to eliminate them, but to recognize them in the wild — is foundational rationalist training.

name: The Internet's Physical Layer
description: The internet feels abstract and invisible, but it runs on 400+ submarine cables stretching across the ocean floor, totaling over 1.3 million kilometers. These fiber-optic cables carry 99% of all international internet traffic as pulses of light. Each cable is roughly the thickness of a garden hose and can carry terabits per second. They are laid and repaired by specialized ships. A single anchor strike, fishing trawl, or shark bite can cut a cable carrying a significant fraction of a country's internet traffic. The 2022 Tonga earthquake severed that island's only submarine cable, cutting the country off from the global internet for weeks. Understanding the physical infrastructure reveals the fragility behind the internet's apparent omnipresence — and why 'the cloud' is, physically, somebody else's building full of servers, connected by tubes at the bottom of the ocean.
